text: The surgeon inspects the pelvic region to identify any endometriosis lesions on the bowel, the pouch of Douglas, and the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to make an incision at the anti-mesial point of the ovary, carefully opening the cyst without using energy to minimize the risk of ovarian failure.

The surgeon measures the ovarian endometriosis cyst at three centimeters and proceeds to remove the menstrual blood from within the cyst. Following this, a cystoscopy is performed to inspect for any vegetation inside the cyst after it has been washed.
The surgeon observes the interior of the ovarian cyst, confirming the absence of vegetation, and prepares to begin the cystectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs traction and counter-traction on the ovarian cyst, aiming to remove the cyst while minimizing damage to the ovary. The surgical instruments are positioned closely to ensure precise manipulation.

The surgeon carefully progresses centimeter by centimeter to locate the appropriate blade for cyst removal, avoiding the use of energy to minimize the risk of ovarian failure during the fertility-sparing cystectomy.

The surgeon uses two devices to apply traction on both the cyst and the ovary, progressing centimeter by centimeter. The dissection is bloodless while in the first and second levels of the cyst. Approaching the third part near the hilum may result in bleeding.

The surgeon controls hemostasis by applying compression to the tissue, avoiding the use of monopolar or bipolar energy. The visual focus is on following the anatomical plane between the cyst and the ovary, guided by an arrow.
The surgeon gently tracks the cyst at level two, approaching the heel of the ovary, and carefully removes the cyst from the ovarian cortex with smooth, precise movements.

The surgeon uses two closely positioned devices to perform traction and counter-traction on the cyst, carefully adjusting the axis of traction to avoid damaging the ovarian cortex. The dissection is performed meticulously to maintain a bloodless field.

The surgeon approaches the hilum of the ovary, where capillary bleeding is visible. Gentle traction and counter-traction are applied to carefully dissect the cyst from the ovarian cortex without using energy devices, aiming to preserve the ovarian reserve.

The surgeon changes the traction technique, with the cyst being pulled by the surgeon and the ovarian cortex by the assistant. Near the hilum, a small bleeder is visible. The plane between the cyst and the ovarian cortex is clearly defined, and a follicle is visible. This marks the final stage of the cystectomy.
The surgeon adjusts the axis of traction between the cyst and the ovary using two closely positioned devices, enhancing the view of the working plane for precise dissection.
The surgeon applies very gentle traction to separate a strong adhesion between the cyst and the ovary, carefully working to preserve the ovarian function by maintaining a precise dissection plane.
The surgeon extracts a large nine-centimeter cyst through a twelve-millimeter trocar. Hemostasis is achieved using a monofilament stitch, specifically a monocryl stitch, to control bleeding.
The surgeon sutures the edge of the ovary, carefully stitching around the entire ovary. The knot is locked gently to prevent ischemia, ensuring the preservation of ovarian blood flow.
The surgeon places stitches to ensure good contact between the two surfaces of the ovary, aiming to reduce the risk of intra-ovarian hematoma.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring the knot is not tied too tightly to maintain good contact with the edges of the ovary. The stitch is then cut, followed by peritoneal washing.
